title: Flowing Gradients Through Hidden Layers
concept: Hidden neurons do not see the final label directly, so they learn by receiving passed-back blame from later layers based on how much they helped shape the wrong prediction.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What can an activation function do to the backward signal?

options.text: Let it pass strongly, weaken it, or block it.
Replace it with a new dataset.
Turn every wrong prediction into a correct one instantly.

exercises.explain: Correct! Activations act like gates during backpropagation.

cards.title: Solving a mistake by tracing it backward
cards.body: A school play goes wrong on stage. The final scene collapses, but the problem probably started earlier — maybe a prop team missed a cue, or a costume change was late. To fix it, you trace the mistake backward through everyone who contributed.

cards.title: Hidden layers pass blame backward
cards.body: A hidden layer does not compare itself directly to the true answer. Instead, it receives a backward signal from the layer after it. Each hidden neuron gets a share of the blame based on how much it influenced the next layer's mistake.

cards.title: Pulling one thread through a sweater
cards.body: If you spot a loose loop on the outside of a sweater, you can trace the thread inward to see which earlier stitch caused it. Hidden layers are like those earlier stitches: they are not the final visible mistake, but they helped create it.

cards.title: A hidden neuron that overreacted to whiskers
cards.body: Suppose a photo classifier mistakes a fox for a cat. One hidden neuron strongly fired for 'whisker-like lines,' and the output layer leaned too hard on that clue. During backprop, that hidden neuron receives stronger blame because its activation helped push the wrong final answer.

cards.title: Hidden neurons are not ignored
cards.body: Because hidden layers never see the true label directly, some people imagine they learn nothing specific. In reality, they still learn through passed-back blame. That is exactly how deep features become useful over time.

cards.title: Direct error vs. passed-back blame
cards.body: Different layers learn from the same mistake in different ways:

left.text:
Gets feedback directly from the loss.
Knows the final prediction was wrong.
Feels the error first.

right.text:
Gets feedback from the next layer.
Learns how much it contributed.
Passes blame farther backward.

exercises.prompt: How does a hidden neuron learn during backpropagation?

options.text: It receives a share of blame from the next layer based on how much it contributed to the final mistake.
It reads the true label directly from the dataset and ignores the output layer.
It updates only when the batch size changes.
It never learns because it is hidden.

exercises.explain: Correct. Hidden neurons learn from the backward signal passed to them by later layers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a hidden neuron had a big influence on a wrong final prediction, it should usually receive a stronger backward signal than a hidden neuron that barely mattered.
exercises.explain: True! Neurons that contributed more to the mistake should receive more blame.

exercises.sentence: Hidden layers learn by passing ___ backward from later layers.
exercises.explain: Blame is the intuition here: the backward signal tells earlier neurons how much they contributed.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best shows a hidden neuron receiving backward blame?

options.text: A hidden neuron strongly activated for 'round eyes,' the output layer wrongly predicted 'owl,' and backprop sends that hidden neuron a correction because it helped push the mistake.
A hidden neuron exports itself as a PNG image after training.
A data engineer renames the training file on disk.

exercises.explain: Exactly. The hidden neuron gets blamed because its activation helped shape the wrong final decision.

exercises.prompt: A medical image network falsely predicts 'tumor.' In the last hidden layer, Neuron X fired very strongly for a bright edge pattern that pushed the output toward 'tumor,' while Neuron Y barely changed. During backpropagation, what should happen?

options.text: Neuron X should receive more backward blame than Neuron Y because it had a larger role in the wrong prediction.
Neuron Y should receive more blame because weaker activations are always more suspicious.
Neither hidden neuron receives any feedback because only the output layer learns.
Both neurons should be deleted and replaced with random ones.

exercises.explain: Exactly right. Backprop traces the mistake through the hidden layers, assigning stronger correction to neurons that mattered more. Next, we will zoom in on a parameter people forget about: the bias term.
